coredata.dc:title: A novel multiemissive Ln/covalent-organic frameworks for ratiometric detection of 2,6-dipicolinic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kuang, Lijuan
Du, Yue
Wang, Shiqi
Yang, Li
Song, Yonghai
coredata.dc:description: As one of the specific biomarkers of anthrax, timely, sensitive and accurate detection of 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) plays an important role in preventing biological weapons attacks and disease outbreaks. Here, multiemission Eu3+/covalent- organic framework (COFDTA-TFP) and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP were constructed by the coordination between Eu3+ or Tb3+ with fluorescent COFDTA-TFP which was prepared by amine-aldehyde dehydration condensation between 2,5-diaminoterephthalic acid and 2,4,6-triformylphloroglucinol for the first time. The Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP can emit non-overlapping fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP and Eu3+ or Tb3+, which was used to ratiometric detection of DPA. Due to the strong coordination between the nitrogen atoms in the pyridine ring and the oxygen atoms in the carboxyl group of DPA with Eu3+ or Tb3+, DPA replaced coordinated H2O to sensitize fluorescence of Eu3+ or Tb3+ by “antenna” effect but fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP with two-dimensional lamellar structure was kept constant as a reference. The linear range and detection limit of ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP for detection of DPA are 0.01–12 µM and 4.2 nM, respectively. The linear range and detection limit of ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP for detection of DPA are 0.01–9.0 µM and 2.9 nM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent-organic framework
Lanthanide ions
2,6-Dipicolinic acid
Multiemission
Ratiometric fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP
Structural characterization of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP
Optical properties of COFDTA-TFP and Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP for DPA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Experimental (black) and refined (red) PXRD patterns of COFDTA-TFP, difference plot between the experimental and refined PXRD patterns (blue). (b) Experimental (black) PXRD and simulated PXRD patterns for AA structure (red), AB structure (blue) of COFDTA-TFP. (c) Eclipsed conformations of COFDTA-TFP. (d) π-π accumulation distance between the two adjacent layers of COFDTA-TFP.

(a) FT-IR spectra of DTA, TFP, COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP. (b) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (c) XRD patterns of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP. (d,e,f) SEM images and (g,h,i) EDS spectra of COFDTA-TFP (d,g), Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP (e,h) and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP (f, i).

(a) Fluorescent spectra of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP + DPA under excitation at 270 nm. (b) Fluorescent spectra of COFDTA-TFP, Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP + DPA under excitation at 270 nm (Excitation slit widths were 10 nm. The temperature was room temperature).

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and (d) Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP in the presence of DPA with different concentration. Fitting curves of the fluorescence intensity of (b) Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and (e) Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP versus DPA concentration. (c) Effect of various metal ions, anion, cysteine and uric acid on the I 615/I 457 of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP. The concentration of all interfere cations is 50 μM except for DPA (5 μM). (f) Effect of various metal ions, anion, cysteine and uric acid on the I 544/I 457 of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP. The concentration of all interfere cations is 80 μM except for DPA (8 μM).
Synthesis route of Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP and schematic diagram of detection of DPA based on Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP.

head.ce:title: A novel multiemissive Ln/covalent-organic frameworks for ratiometric detection of 2,6-dipicolinic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the specific biomarkers of anthrax, timely, sensitive and accurate detection of 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) plays an important role in preventing biological weapons attacks and disease outbreaks. Here, multiemission Eu3+/covalent- organic framework (COFDTA-TFP) and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP were constructed by the coordination between Eu3+ or Tb3+ with fluorescent COFDTA-TFP which was prepared by amine-aldehyde dehydration condensation between 2,5-diaminoterephthalic acid and 2,4,6-triformylphloroglucinol for the first time. The Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP can emit non-overlapping fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP and Eu3+ or Tb3+, which was used to ratiometric detection of DPA. Due to the strong coordination between the nitrogen atoms in the pyridine ring and the oxygen atoms in the carboxyl group of DPA with Eu3+ or Tb3+, DPA replaced coordinated H2O to sensitize fluorescence of Eu3+ or Tb3+ by “antenna” effect but fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP with two-dimensional lamellar structure was kept constant as a reference. The linear range and detection limit of ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP for detection of DPA are 0.01–12 µM and 4.2 nM, respectively. The linear range and detection limit of ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP for detection of DPA are 0.01–9.0 µM and 2.9 nM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax bacteria are widely found in soil, water and air. Anthrax bacteria can cause disease and food poisoning, and can also be used as biological weapons in terrorist attacks, so the detection of Anthrax bacteria is of great significance [1]. 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) accounts for about 5–15% of the total mass of dried Bacillus anthracis and does not exist in other common spores. Therefore, the timely, sensitive and accurate detection of DPA plays a vital role in the prevention of biological weapons attacks and disease outbreaks [2].

Current methods for the detection of DPA, including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [3], chromatography [4], electrochemical sensing [5], mass spectrometry [6], etc., have more or less disadvantages such as cumbersome operation, expensive reagent, complex sample processing, etc., which limit their practical application. Compared with these traditional detection methods, ratiometric fluorescence sensor has attracted intense attention because of its high sensitivity, high selectivity and fast response [7–9]. Lanthanide ions (Ln3+) have been extensively used to determine DPA due to their outstanding luminescent properties such as large Stokes shift, sharp f-f emission bands, long fluorescent life and high quantum yields [10–14]. However, in aqueous solution, the coordination of water molecules with Ln3+ will result in fluorescence quenching of Ln3+ [15–17]. Another major disadvantage is that their absorption coefficients of Ln3+ are very low and accordingly they always show no emission or weak emission [18,19]. To overcome these drawbacks, Ln3+ is prepared into Ln3+-complex in which the coordinated water is replaced by ligand and Ln3+ is excited through a so-called “antenna” ligand [20–22]. Nevertheless, Ln3+-complexes also suffer from poor light stability, thermal stability and mechanical properties, and tend to aggregate in a solution [23–25]. The aggregation can be avoided by popularly tailoring the Ln3+ coordination polymer (Ln3+-CPs) or Ln3+ metal–organic frameworks (Ln3+-MOFs) which are porous materials (crystalline materials for Ln3+-MOFs) and show great potential applications [26–31]. It is regrettable that their poor stability limits their wide applications. Another good solution is designing organic–inorganic hybrid materials, where a Ln3+ or Ln3+-complex is used as the guest and organic or inorganic materials as the main matrix. The supporting matrix should have many functional groups to replace as much coordinated water molecules as possible which can broaden their applications, as well as high stability, large specific surface area and high porosity which is conducive to mass transfer.

Covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) are a class of conjugated crystalline polymer with large specific surface area, adjustable pore size, structure and functional groups, and high porosity [32–37]. They are connected by covalent bonds, among which the most common covalent bonds mainly include imine bond, borate bond and triazine bond [38–41]. Among these covalent bonds, COFs based on imine bond has remarkable stability, which has attracted the interest of many scientists [41–45]. Porous COFs with various functions can be designed by regulating monomers with different functional groups. COFs usually have two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) porous crystalline structure. Compared with Ln3+-CPs and Ln3+-MOFs, the imine-linked COFs showed remarkable chemical, light, thermal and mechanical stability. The excellent advantages indicate the imine-linked COFs should be an excellent matrix to support Ln3+ or Ln3+-complex. If the COFs used as a matrix can also emit fluorescence, it is possible to construct a multiemission composite material, which will be very attractive because they can be used for ratiometric fluorescence sensing and visible detection. However, because of its rigid structure and less functional groups, only a few Ln3+-complex can be grafted onto COFs to construct Ln3+-complex/COFs organic–inorganic hybrid materials.

In this work, Eu3+ or Tb3+ were used to coordinate with COFDTA-TFP to construct multiemission Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP due to the non-overlapping fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP and Eu3+ or Tb3+. COFDTA-TFP was prepared by amine-aldehyde dehydration condensation between 2,5-diaminoterephthalic acid (DTA) and 2,4,6-triformylphloroglucinol (TFP). The COFDTA-TFP has abundant functional groups including –COOH, –OH, –CO and –NH, and good flexibility because the imine bond was transformed into secondary amine, which facilitates its coordination with the Eu3+ or Tb3+. The Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP hybrid materials can emit non-overlapping fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP and Eu3+ or Tb3+, which was used to ratiometric detection of DPA. Due to the strong coordination between the nitrogen atoms on the pyridine ring and the oxygen atoms in the carboxyl group of DPA with Eu3+ or Tb3+, DPA replaced coordinated H2O to sensitize the fluorescence of Eu3+ or Tb3+ by the “antenna” effect but the fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP was kept constant as a reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP
ce:section.ce:para: The chemicals used were shown in Supporting Information. For preparation of COFDTA-TFP, 0.03 mmol DTA and 0.02 mmol TFP were firstly dissolved in 4 mL DMF and then 0.4 mL 6 mol L−1 acetic acid was added. After complete dissolution, the mixture was transferred to a reaction kettle with a volume of 20 mL and placed in an oven at 120 °C for 3 days. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally, the precipitation was obtained by centrifugation at the rate of 10,000 r.m.p. for 10 min. DMF was added to wash the product, and the above operation was repeated at least three times. After centrifugation, the yellow product was obtained and dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain COFDTA-TFP. In order to obtain 2D COF nanosheets with good dispersibility, COFs was fully ground in an agate mortar. The preparation procedures of fluorescent COFDTA-TFP are shown in Scheme 1 .

For preparation of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP or Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP, 20 mg dried ground 2D COFDTA-TFP nanosheets and 5 mg Eu(NO3)3·6H2O or Tb(NO3)3·6H2O were evenly stirred in 5 mL DMF for 3 days. The products were centrifuged at the rate of 10,000 r.m.p. for 10 min, then the products were washed with DMF, and the above operation was repeated at least three times. After centrifugation, the yellow product was obtained and dried in an oven at 60 °C for later use. The preparation procedures are shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP
ce:section.ce:para: Various characterization methods used were shown in Supporting Information. The powder X-ray powder diffraction (PXRD) pattern of COFDTA-TFP showed peaks at 5.78°, 15.56°, 18.07° and 24.42°, corresponding to faces (020), (100), (101), (111) and (110), respectively, indicating that the COFDTA-TFP had good crystallinity (Fig. 1 a). The simulated PXRD pattern was highly consistent with experimental results (RWP = 4.32%, RWP = 3.63%). The results indicated that COFDTA-TFP is P1 space group, in which cell parameters are A = 20.3 Å, B = 23.1 Å, C = 4.1 Å, α = β = 90°, γ = 120°. Compared with the simulated AA and AB pattern, the experimental PXRD of COFDTA-TFP has a better coincidence with the AB pattern (Fig. 1b). The large size of COOH will hinder the interaction between adjacent layers to form the arrangement of the overlapping AA-superposition model. So COFDTA-TFP is more inclined to follow the overlapping AB-superposition model (Fig. S1). The 3D structure of COFDTA-TFP showed clearly that the as-synthesized COFDTA-TFP was a hexagonal hcb structure (Fig. 1c) [46]. Calculation results showed that the distance between the two adjacent layers in the COFDTA-TFP was 2.06 Å (Fig. 1d).

It could be observed from Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum that the characteristic vibration spectrum peaks of N–H and O–H in COFDTA-TFP were located at 3433 cm−1 and 3343 cm−1, respectively, the stretching vibration spectrum peaks of CO appeared at 1707 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1, and the characteristic spectrum bands of C–N appeared at 1254 cm−1 (Fig. 2 a), indicating the imine bond was transformed into secondary amine and –COOH was preserved [47,48]. The transformation from imine bond to secondary amine could increase the flexibility of COFDTA-TFP, which facilitated the coordination of functional groups in COFDTA-TFP with Eu3+ or Tb3+. The position of peaks in the FT-IR spectra of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP remained basically unchanged after the coordination of Eu3+ and Tb3+ with COFDTA-TFP. It was found that the characteristic diffraction peaks of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP were basically consistent, indicating that the coordination of Eu3+ and Tb3+ with COFDTA-TFP did not change the lattice structure, and had no effect on the skeleton structure of COFDTA-TFP materials (Fig. 2c). Further, the possible structure of Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP were optimized at the B3LYP/6-311G** level of density functional theory. Since COFDTA-TFP has a large number of atoms, a structural unit was used for calculation. According to the calculation results, the keto state of COFDTA-TFP is 0.08 eV more stable than the enol state. The keto form acts as a tridentate ligand and Eu3+ strongly coordinates to the electron dense region of COFDTA-TFP (–COOH, –CO and –NH2) (Fig. S2).

Subsequently, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further prove the binding mode and binding sites of COFDTA-TFP and Ln3+. XPS spectrum of COFDTA-TFP showed C, N and O elements (Fig. S3c). Ln elements appeared in XPS diagram of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP (Fig. S3a and b). As shown in Fig. S3, the peak positions of Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2 were 1134.2 and 1163.7 eV, respectively. The peak positions of Tb 3d5/2 and Tb 3d3/2 were 1242 and 1276 eV, respectively. The result indicated that the valence state of Ln was +3. It was also proved that lanthanide and COFDTA-TFP coordinated successfully. Fig. S4 were XPS O1s spectra and N1s spectra of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP. In the XPS O1 spectra of COFDTA-TFP (Fig. S4a), the two peaks at 532.8 and 531.1 eV corresponded to the C–O–H and CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4a–c, after binding with Ln3+, both peaks were offset to some extent (Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP: +0.3 eV, +0.2 eV; Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP: +0.7 eV, +0.4 eV), indicating that the formation of coordination bonds (Ln–O) [49,50]. In N1s XPS spectra of COFDTA-TFP (Fig. S4d), the two peaks at 398.8 eV (N–C/N–H) and 400.8 eV (NC) corresponded to the nitrogen atoms in the keto and enol states, respectively. After combining with Ln3+, both peaks shifted towards high binding energy (Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP: +1.0 eV, +0.7 eV; Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP: +1.3 eV, +1.0 eV), and the contribution rate of N–C/N–H to the whole N 1s XPS signal was significantly increased, indicating that N atom was one of the binding sites, and Ln3+ was more inclined to bind to the keto state of COFDTA-TFP (Fig. S4d–f) [51,52].

According to N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of COFDTA-TFP, Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP, the specific surface areas of these three materials were 92.49 m2 g−1, 97.64 m2 g−1 and 90.26 m2 g−1 (Fig. 2b), respectively, indicating that the specific surface areas of COFDTA-TFP remained basically unchanged after loading with Tb3+ and Eu3+. The pore diameter of COFDTA-TFP is 3.87 nm, which is not too far with calculation results (Fig. S1). And the pore diameter of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP are 3.67 nm and 3.61 nm, respectively, indicating that the coordination of Eu3+ and Tb3+ in COFDTA-TFP holes only leads to a slight decrease in pore size (Fig. S5).

It could be observed from scanning electron microscope (SEM) images that the COFDTA-TFP (Fig. 2d), Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP (Fig. 2e) and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP (Fig. 2f) were all 2D fold lamellar structures. After the coordination of Eu3+ and Tb3+ with COFDTA-TFP, the structure of 2D COFDTA-TFP nanosheet structures was not affected. EDS spectra of COFDTA-TFP showed C, N, O elements (Fig. 2g), while Eu elements and Tb elements appeared in EDS spectra of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP (Fig. 2h) and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP (Fig. 2i), respectively. The content of Eu3+ in Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+ in Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP were about 20% and 21%, respectively. EDX element mappings of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP (Fig. S6) and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP (Fig. S7) showed that Eu3+ and Tb3+ are evenly distributed on the surface of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP. The thermal stability of these materials was determined by thermal analysis of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP. As shown in Fig. S8, the weight loss of the two materials could be divided into two different stages as the temperature increased. The first stage of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP (30–220 °C) was due to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water and solvent remaining on the outside and/or inside the materials. The most significant weight loss occurred in the second stage (≥220 °C), which suggested that the basic skeleton of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP was destroyed and decomposed. The thermal analysis of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP was basically consistent with that of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP. The residual mass of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP was 41.5% and 40.2% respectively, when approaching 900 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of COFDTA-TFP and Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 a (black curve), the COFDTA-TFP exhibited dual emission at 456 nm and 550 nm at the excitation wavelength of 270 nm (Fig. S9). To determine the origin of the dual emission, fluorescence spectra of COFDTA-TFP in ethanol with different water contents were determined (Fig. S10). With the increase of water content, hydrogen bonds were formed between hydroxyl group and water molecule, resulting in the change of enol structure and the decrease of the emission of enol state. However, as the structure of keto was remained stable in water, the emission of keto state was also remained stable [53]. It was well-known that when DPA was coordinated with Ln3+, DPA sensitized the fluorescence of Ln3+ through absorption-energy transfer-emission effect. So, after the addition of DPA, due to the coordination of Eu3+ or Tb3+ with DPA produced the “antenna” effect, DPA transferred energy to Eu3+ or Tb3+ to sensitize the fluorescence of Eu3+ (green curve in Fig. 3a) or Tb3+ (green curve in Fig. 3b). The emission peaks of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP at 592 nm, 615 nm and 693 nm might be due to the characteristic transition between the energy levels of Eu3+ from 5D0 to 7FJ (J = 1, 2, and 3) [54,55]. The fluorescence emission peaks of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP at 489 nm, 544 nm, 584 nm and 621 nm were caused by the characteristic transitions of Tb3+ from 5D4 to 7FJ (J = 6, 5, 4, and 3) [56–58]. In Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP, DPA basically had no effect on the fluorescence peak of COFDTA-TFP at 456 nm.

As shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence intensity of characteristic peak of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP was significantly affected by the excitation light with different wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength was 270 nm, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP material was the strongest at 615 nm. Similarly, under the excitation wavelength of 270 nm, Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP generated the strongest fluorescence at 544 nm. Therefore, Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP could be excited by the light of 270 nm. As shown in Fig. S12a, the fluorescence of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP increased significantly with the increase of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP concentration. When the concentration of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP was 0.012 mg mL−1, the fluorescence of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP arrived at the maximum value (Fig. S12b). After that, the fluorescence intensity was basically kept constant. The Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP also showed a similar result but the maximum fluorescence of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP achieved at 0.016 mg mL−1 (Fig. S12c and d). In addition, compared with the Ln3+ and Ln3+/DPA, the Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP constructed by Ln3+ and COFDTA-TFP coordination reduced the coordination of Ln3+ to water, and accordingly its fluorescence performance was best (Fig. S13). Compared with Ln3+/DTA and Ln3+/TFP, the fluorescence of Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP is stronger and more stable, which is beneficial to ratiometric fluorescence detection (Fig. S14). After storage for 3 days, the results showed that Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP in a solution did not aggregate, which obviously was superior to Ln3+-complex and naked Ln3+ (Fig. S13). It was because naked Ln3+ was easy to hydrolyze and precipitate in aqueous solution, and the coordination of Ln3+ with COFDTA-TFP inhibited the hydrolysis of naked Ln3+. Besides, Eu3+ or Tb3+ was uniformly assembled into pore of COFDTA-TFP, which effectively prevented them from hydrolyzing. Simultaneously, the assembly of Ln3+ gave Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP lot of positive charges. Under electrostatic repulsion among positive charges, the Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP could be well dispersed in a solution at high concentration.

Fig. S15 showed the effect of the pH of NaAc-HAc buffer solution on the reaction between Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP and DPA. When Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP composite and DPA were dispersed in NaAc-HAc buffer solution with a pH of 3–10, the fluorescence of Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP was changed as the pH of the buffer solution. Since I 615/I 457 of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and I 544/I 457 of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP remained stable at pH = 6–9 and pH = 6–8, detection was performed in NaAc-HAc buffer solution at pH = 7.5 and pH = 7, respectively (Fig. S15). The results clearly showed that under strong acid/base conditions, the Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP-DPA composite also showed good fluorescence, indicating the Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP could be used under strong acid/base conditions. The good acid/base stability should originate from the excellent stability of COFDTA-TFP and the strong coordination between Ln3+ and COFDTA-TFP. Usually, the naked Ln3+ was more inclined to react with OH– to form precipitation under alkaline conditions, and Ln-MOFs was more prone to collapse under strong acid conditions [59]. Therefore, the Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP was obviously superior to Ln3+ and Ln-MOFs.

The reaction time of DPA with Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S16a and b, after DPA was added, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ in Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP increased continuously but basically remained unchanged after 12 min, indicating that Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and DPA almost completely reacted in 12 min. Similarly, as shown in Fig. S16c and d, the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ in Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP sensitized by DPA did not change significantly after 14 min, indicating that the Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP reached a complete reaction equilibrium with DPA. The long reaction time indicated that the Eu3+ and Tb3+ might diffuse into the interior of the COFDTA-TFP nanosheet to coordinate with COFDTA-TFP. The DPA might also diffuse into the pore of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP to replace the coordinated water of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP, which resulted in the long reaction time. However, the pore of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP could screen out some structurally similar interferences. As shown in Fig. S17, after the addition of DPA, the XPS 3d peak at 1134.2 eV and 1163.7 eV from the Eu3+ within Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP was shifted to a lower binding energy (1133.6 eV and 1163.0 eV) and the 3d peak at 1242 eV and 1276 eV from the Tb3+ within Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP was shifted to a lower binding energy (1241.5 eV and 1276.5 eV), revealing the bonding between Ln3+ and DPA. This was because the formed coordination bonds (Ln–O and Ln–N) led to an increase in the Ln electron density while a decrease in the binding energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP for DPA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized experimental conditions, the DPA was detected using a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP. As shown in Fig. 4 a, as the DPA concentration increased from 0.01 to 12.0 µM, the fluorescence emission peak of COFDTA-TFP at 457 nm basically remained unchanged, while the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ increased gradually. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 615/I 457 presented a good linear relationship with DPA concentration from 0.01 to 12.0 µM (R2 = 0.996). In addition, the detection limit of DPA was about 4.2 nM when the SNR was 3. The Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP was also used to detect DPA. The emission peak of COFDTA-TFP at 457 nm remained unchanged, and the fluorescence emission peak of Tb3+ gradually increased with the increase of DPA concentration (Fig. 4d). Among the four emission peaks of Tb3+, the strongest emission peak at 544 nm was selected as the detection signal of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP fluorescence sensor for DPA detection. As shown in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 544/I 457 presented a good linear relationship with DPA concentration from 0.01 to 9.0 µM (R2 = 0.999) When the SNR was 3, the detection limit was as low as 2.9 nM, indicating that the Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP ratiometric fluorescence sensor was relatively sensitive. Compared with other DPA fluorescence sensors, the Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP had a wider detection range (Table S1). In addition, compared with other sensors [60–64], the detection limit of Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP was also lower. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP was more reasonable to detect DPA.

Only the probe with good selectivity could be used to accurately detect the object. Therefore, in this work, the selectivity of the ratiometric Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP fluorescent probe was studied. Firstly, various metal ions Mn+ (Mn+ = Mg2+, Ag+, Sn2+, Hg2+, K+), anion Xn− (Xn− = F−, Cl−, Br−, I−), cysteine and uric acid, which might interfere with DPA detection, were mixed with DPA respectively, and then the fluorescence of the mixture was determined by fluorescence spectroscopy. In the experiments, the concentration of various interferences was 10 times that of DPA. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence of the probe was significantly enhanced after adding 5.0 µM DPA to Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP solution. After adding different 50 µM other substances, these substances had little effect on the I 615/I 457. The same experimental phenomenon was also found in the Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP ratiometric fluorescence sensor. When adding 8.0 µM DPA into Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP solution, the fluorescence of Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP ratiometric probe was significantly enhanced. After adding 80.0 µM different other substances, there was basically no effect on the I 544/I 457 (Fig. 4f). Both experiments showed that Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP had excellent selectivity for DPA detection. It is possible that there was a strong characteristic coordination between the nitrogen atom of DPA pyridine ring and the oxygen atom in the carboxyl group on the pyridine ring and Ln3+, so that the coordination between them was not disturbed by other substances. Furthermore, the ratiometric method also could resist the interference of many operating factors and environmental factors. Therefore, the fluorescence sensor based on Ln3+/COFDTA-TFP had high selectivity and good stability for the detection of DPA.

To sum up, a new fluorescent COFDTA-TFP with dual emission was prepared by Schiff base reaction using DTA and TFP as monomers based on the second amine-linkage which resulted in good flexibility. The COFDTA-TFP presented 2D nanosheets with functional group and good flexibility, which facilitated the coordination of Eu3+ or Tb3+ with it to construct multiemission Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP. After the coordination, the dual emission became single emission but the fluorescence was enhanced. The multiemission Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP could be used in high concentration, which was superior to naked Eu3+ or Tb3+ and their complex. The DPA could enter into the pores of Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP to sensitize the fluorescence of Eu3+ or Tb3+ through antenna effect, but the fluorescence of COFDTA-TFP was kept constant. So, the multiemission Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP was used for ratiometric detection of DPA. Since COFDTA-TFP contained a large number of functional groups, and only a small amount of water coordinated with Eu3+ or Tb3+, the fluorescence of Eu3+ or Tb3+ could be sensitized with only a small amount of DPA, which made the ratiometric fluorescence sensor have a high sensitivity. The linear range and detection limit of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP for the detection of DPA were 0.01–12.0 µM and 4.2 nM, respectively. The linear range and detection limit of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP for the detection of DPA were 0.01–9.0 µM and 2.9 nM, respectively. The Eu3+/COFDTA-TFP and Tb3+/COFDTA-TFP ratiometric sensors also showed a good selectivity and stability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665012 and 21765009), and Natural Science Foundation from Department of Education of the JiangXi Province (GJJ200320).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2021.10.022.

ce:section.ce:para:
